q: Trace a piece of undigested food from the mouth to the anus.
a: Mouth (Oral cavity) to Pharynx to Esophagus to Stomach to Small Intestine (Duodenum, Jejunum, Ileum) to Large Intestine (Colon) to Rectum to Anus.

q: Define the following keywords: gastrointestinal tract (GI tract), gut, and chyme.
a: GI tract: The long tube from mouth to anus. Gut: Often refers to the stomach and intestines. Chyme: The soupy mixture of food and secretions created in the stomach.

q: Define the following keywords: oral cavity, salivary glands, and esophagus.
a: Oral cavity: The mouth where mechanical digestion begins. Salivary glands: Glands that secrete saliva and enzymes. Esophagus: The muscular tube connecting the pharynx to the stomach.

q: Define the following keywords: stomach, pancreas, and liver.
a: Stomach: Muscular organ for storage and mixing. Pancreas: Organ providing digestive enzymes and bicarbonate. Liver: Organ producing bile and processing nutrients.

q: Define the following keywords: small intestine, duodenum, jejunum, and ileum.
a: Small intestine: Primary site of digestion and absorption. Duodenum: First part (c-shaped). Jejunum: Middle part. Ileum: Final part connecting to large intestine.

q: Define the following keywords: large intestine, colon, feces, rectum, and anus.
a: Large intestine: Absorbs water and stores waste. Colon: Proximal portion of large intestine. Feces: Solid waste. Rectum: Distal storage for feces. Anus: Inferior exit of GI tract.

q: Describe the layers of the GI tract wall: Mucosa.
a: The innermost layer consisting of mucosal epithelium, lamina propria (connective tissue with lymph/nerves), and muscularis mucosae (thin smooth muscle).

q: Describe the layers of the GI tract wall: Submucosa.
a: Connective tissue layer deep to the mucosa containing larger blood/lymph vessels and the submucosal plexus (nerve network).

q: Describe the layers of the GI tract wall: Muscularis externa.
a: Outer muscle layer consisting of an inner circular layer (for narrowing) and an outer longitudinal layer (for shortening).

q: Describe the layers of the GI tract wall: Serosa.
a: The outermost layer of the GI wall, which is a continuation of the peritoneal membrane (peritoneum).

q: Define the following keywords: villi, gastric glands, and crypts.
a: Villi: Finger-like projections in small intestine mucosa. Gastric glands: Deep pits in the stomach wall for secretion. Crypts: Deep pits in the intestinal wall for secretion.

q: Define the following keywords: submucosal glands, mucosal epithelium, and lamina propria.
a: Submucosal glands: Glands in the submucosa. Mucosal epithelium: Innermost cell layer (absorptive or secretory). Lamina propria: Connective tissue supporting epithelium.

q: Define the following keywords: muscularis mucosae, mucosal vs serosal surface.
a: Muscularis mucosae: Smooth muscle at mucosal base. Mucosal surface: Faces the lumen. Serosal surface: Faces the abdominal cavity.

q: Describe the primary function of the digestive system.
a: To move nutrients, water, and electrolytes from the external environment into the body's internal environment.

q: Describe and compare the process of secretion.
a: The movement of water and ions from the ECF to the GI lumen, or the release of synthesized substances by GI epithelial cells.

q: Describe and compare the process of digestion.
a: The chemical and mechanical breakdown of food into smaller units that can be taken across the intestinal epithelium.

q: Describe and compare the process of absorption.
a: The active or passive movement of substances from the GI lumen to the ECF (extracellular fluid).

q: Describe and compare the process of motility.
a: Movement of material through the GI tract as a result of muscle contraction.

q: Discuss the role of mass balance within the digestive system.
a: Matching fluid intake (oral and secretions) with fluid output (absorption and excretion) to maintain homeostatic volume.

q: Explain the challenge of autodigestion in the digestive system.
a: The risk of digestive enzymes (like proteases) breaking down the body's own tissues; solved by secreting enzymes as inactive proenzymes.

q: Explain the challenge of mass balance in the digestive system.
a: Replacing the nearly 7-9 liters of fluid secreted daily; failure to reabsorb leads to dehydration.

q: Explain the challenge of defense in the digestive system.
a: The GI tract is a massive surface area open to the environment; solved by mucus, digestive acid, and GALT (Gut-Associated Lymphoid Tissue).

q: Discuss the role of digestive enzymes within the process of digestion.
a: Proteins that catalyze the chemical breakdown of biomolecules; often secreted into the lumen or bound to apical membranes (brush border).

q: Discuss the role of mucus within the process of digestion.
a: A viscous secretion (mucins) that forms a protective coating over GI epithelium and lubricates the movement of food contents.

q: Describe and compare peristalsis.
a: Progressive waves of contraction that move a bolus forward; circular muscles contract just behind the mass to push it.

q: Describe and compare segmentation.
a: Short segments of intestine contract and relax to mix contents and bring them into contact with the absorptive epithelium.

q: Describe and compare the migrating motor complex (MMC).
a: A series of contractions that occur between meals ('housekeeping') to sweep remnants and bacteria from the stomach to the large intestine.

q: Describe the intestinal phase of digestion.
a: The phase where chyme enters the small intestine and triggers secretory and motor responses via feedback and feedforward mechanisms.

q: Explain how the anatomy of the small intestine facilitates secretion, digestion, and absorption.
a: Plicae, villi, and microvilli dramatically increase surface area; crypts and glands provide secretory capacity.

q: Understand the role of the pancreas in endocrine function.
a: Islet cells secrete hormones (insulin and glucagon) directly into the blood to regulate metabolism.

q: Understand the role of the pancreas in exocrine function.
a: Acini and ducts secrete digestive enzymes and bicarbonate into the duodenum via the pancreatic duct.

q: Describe the role of Digestive enzymes in the intestinal phase.
a: Synthesized in inactive forms (zymogens) and activated in the lumen to break down proteins, fats, and carbohydrates.

q: Describe the role of Bile and bile salts in the intestinal phase.
a: Bile salts (amphipathic) emulsify large fat droplets into smaller ones to increase lipase access.

q: Describe the role of Bicarbonate in the intestinal phase.
a: Secreted mainly from the pancreas to neutralize the acidic chyme entering from the stomach.

q: Describe the role of Mucus in the intestinal phase.
a: Secreted by goblet cells to protect the intestinal lining and lubricate contents.

q: Describe the role of Isotonic NaCl solution (CFTR channels) in the intestinal phase.
a: Crypt cells secrete NaCl to provide a watery lubricant for the lumen; uses the CFTR chloride channel.

q: Diagram/Explain the digestion and absorption of fats.
a: Emulsification by bile -> Lipase breakdown to monoglycerides/fatty acids -> Micelle formation -> Diffusion into cell -> Chylomicron formation -> Lacteal absorption.

q: Define the following keywords: amphipathic, lipases, and colipase.
a: Amphipathic: Has both polar and non-polar parts (bile salts). Lipases: Enzymes that digest fats. Colipase: Protein that allows lipase access through bile salts.

q: Define the following keywords: micelles, phospholipase, and chylomicrons.
a: Micelles: Small disks of bile salts and fat products. Phospholipase: Digests phospholipids. Chylomicrons: Large lipoprotein complexes for transport out of the cell.

q: Define the keyword: lacteals.
a: Specialized lymph vessels in intestinal villi that absorb chylomicrons.

q: Diagram/Explain the digestion and absorption of carbohydrates.
a: Amylase breaks down starch to disaccharides; brush border enzymes (maltase, etc.) break these to monosaccharides; absorbed via SGLT and GLUT transporters.

q: Define the keywords: polysaccharides (starch, cellulose, glycogen) and disaccharides (lactose, maltose).
a: Polysaccharides: Long chains (starch/glycogen are digestible; cellulose is not). Disaccharides: Two sugars (lactose is glucose+galactose; maltose is glucose+glucose).

q: Define the keywords: monosaccharides (glucose, fructose), amylase, and fiber.
a: Monosaccharides: Single sugars. Amylase: Enzyme for starch. Fiber: Indigestible plant polysaccharides (cellulose).

q: Define the keywords: lactase, SGLT transporter, GLUT2, and GLUT5.
a: Lactase: Digests lactose. SGLT: Na+-glucose symporter (apical). GLUT2: Facilitated diffusion (basolateral). GLUT5: Fructose transporter (apical).

q: Diagram/Explain the digestion and absorption of proteins.
a: Endopeptidases (pepsin, trypsin) and exopeptidases (carboxypeptidase) break proteins into amino acids or small peptides; absorbed via Na+ or H+ cotransport.

q: Define the following keywords: endopeptidases, proteases, and pepsin.
a: Endopeptidases: Attack internal peptide bonds. Proteases: General enzymes for proteins. Pepsin: Stomach endopeptidase.

q: Define the following keywords: trypsin and exopeptidase.
a: Trypsin: Pancreatic endopeptidase. Exopeptidase: Attacks terminal peptide bonds to release single amino acids.

q: Explain the cellular mechanism for the absorption of water.
a: Occurs by osmosis following the osmotic gradient created by the absorption of solutes (like Na+ and nutrients).

q: Explain the cellular mechanism for the absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
a: Absorbed along with fats in micelles and then incorporated into chylomicrons.

q: Explain the cellular mechanism for the absorption of water-soluble vitamins (C, most B).
a: Absorbed by mediated transport (facilitated diffusion or active transport).

q: Explain the cellular mechanism for the absorption of Vitamin B12 and intrinsic factor.
a: B12 must bind to Intrinsic Factor (secreted by stomach) to be absorbed in the ileum via specific receptors.

q: Explain the cellular mechanism for the absorption of iron and calcium.
a: Regulated according to body needs; iron uses DMT1; calcium uses apical channels and paracellular pathways regulated by Vitamin D.

q: Explain the neural control of the intestinal phase of digestion.
a: Enteric Nervous System (ENS) coordinates local reflexes (short reflexes) and communicates with the CNS (long reflexes) via the vagus nerve.

q: Explain the hormonal control: Secretin.
a: Released by S cells in response to acid; stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion and inhibits gastric emptying/acid.

q: Explain the hormonal control: Cholecystokinin (CCK).
a: Released in response to fats/proteins; stimulates gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion; slows gastric emptying.

q: Explain the hormonal control: GIP and GLP-1.
a: Incretin hormones released in response to carbohydrates; stimulate insulin secretion in anticipation of rising glucose.

q: Define metabolism.
a: The sum of all chemical reactions in the body, including energy extraction, synthesis, and waste removal.

q: Describe the components of energy input in our bodies.
a: Exclusively derived from the nutrients we ingest, absorb, and metabolize (Carbohydrates, Lipids, Proteins).

q: Describe the components of energy output in our bodies.
a: Divided into work (mechanical, chemical, transport) and heat (lost to environment).

q: Define the keywords: transport work and mechanical work.
a: Transport: Moving molecules across membranes. Mechanical: Muscle contraction and movement of organelles.

q: Define the keywords: chemical work, short-term energy storage, and long-term energy storage.
a: Chemical: Growth/synthesis. Short-term: Glycogen (liver/muscle). Long-term: Triglycerides (adipose).

q: Distinguish between anabolic and catabolic pathways.
a: Anabolic: Synthesis of larger molecules from smaller ones (uses energy). Catabolic: Breakdown of larger molecules into smaller ones (releases energy).

q: Distinguish between the fed (absorptive) state and the fasted (postabsorptive) state.
a: Fed state: Period following a meal when nutrients are being absorbed and stored. Fasted state: Period when the body taps into stored energy.

q: Describe the possible fates of ingested carbohydrates.
a: Immediate use for ATP (energy), synthesis of glycogen (storage), or conversion to fat (excess).

q: Describe the possible fates of ingested proteins.
a: Synthesis of body proteins (structural/functional) or conversion to fat/energy if in excess.

q: Describe the possible fates of ingested lipids.
a: Stored as triglycerides in adipose tissue, used for structural membranes/steroids, or used for energy (less common in fed state).

q: Define the following keywords: glycogenesis and lipogenesis.
a: Glycogenesis: Formation of glycogen from glucose. Lipogenesis: Synthesis of fat (triglycerides) from non-lipid precursors.

q: Define the following keywords: glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis.
a: Glycogenolysis: Breakdown of glycogen to glucose. Gluconeogenesis: Synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors (amino acids/glycerol).

q: Explain the regulatory significance of push-pull control.
a: Using different enzymes for forward and reverse directions of a reaction to allow fine-tuned control of metabolic flux.

q: Describe the fed-state of metabolism and what signals its beginning.
a: The state of 'building' (anabolism); signaled by high plasma glucose levels and the secretion of insulin.

q: Identify the fate of amino acids in the fed state (excess).
a: Excess amino acids can be converted into fatty acids or ketone bodies for storage or energy.

q: Explain the fates of carbohydrates in the fed-state.
a: Glucose is used for ATP production (glycolysis), stored as glycogen (glycogenesis), or converted to fat (lipogenesis) if stores are full.

q: Explain the fates of proteins in the fed-state.
a: Amino acids go to the liver for protein synthesis or are sent to other tissues. Excess is converted to fat or ketone bodies.

q: Explain the fates of lipids in the fed-state.
a: Chylomicrons are broken down by lipoprotein lipase; fatty acids are stored as triglycerides in adipose or used for energy/cholesterol.

q: Define the keywords: apoproteins, lipoprotein lipase, and very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL).
a: Apoproteins: Proteins on lipoproteins. Lipoprotein lipase: Enzyme on capillary walls that digests fats in chylomicrons. VLDL: Lipoprotein that carries fats from liver to tissues.

q: Define the keywords: low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and high-density lipoprotein (HDL).
a: LDL-C: 'Bad' cholesterol; carries cholesterol to tissues. HDL: 'Good' cholesterol; carries cholesterol back to the liver for excretion.

q: Define the keywords: apoB, apoA, and hypercholesterolemia.
a: apoB: Protein on LDL. apoA: Protein on HDL. Hypercholesterolemia: High levels of cholesterol in the blood.

q: Explain the relationship between different forms of cholesterol and cardiovascular disease.
a: High LDL-C is a risk factor as it contributes to plaque; high HDL-C is protective as it helps remove cholesterol from the system.

q: Describe the fasted state of metabolism and what signals its beginning.
a: A catabolic state where plasma glucose drops, insulin levels fall, and glucagon rises to tap into energy stores.

q: Explain the fates of carbohydrates in the fasted state.
a: Liver glycogen is broken down (glycogenolysis) to glucose; glucose is also made from non-carbs (gluconeogenesis).

q: Explain the fates of proteins in the fasted state.
a: Body proteins are broken down into amino acids (proteolysis). Amino acids are used for gluconeogenesis.

q: Discuss the relationship between amino acid catabolism and urea.
a: Deamination of amino acids produces ammonia, which is toxic; the liver converts ammonia into urea for excretion by the kidneys.

q: Define the keywords: proteases, deamination, and urea.
a: Proteases: Enzymes that break down proteins. Deamination: Removal of an amino group. Urea: Nitrogenous waste product of protein breakdown.

q: Explain the fates of lipids in the fasted state.
a: Triglycerides are broken down into glycerol and fatty acids (lipolysis). Fatty acids undergo beta-oxidation to produce ATP or ketone bodies.

q: Define the keywords: lipolysis, beta-oxidation, and ketone bodies.
a: Lipolysis: Fat breakdown. Beta-oxidation: Process converting fatty acids to Acetyl CoA. Ketone bodies: Energy molecules produced by the liver from fatty acids.

q: Define the keywords: ketosis and ketoacidosis.
a: Ketosis: High levels of ketones in the blood. Ketoacidosis: A dangerous drop in blood pH due to extreme ketone production (common in Type 1 Diabetes).

q: Explain the role of insulin in the control of metabolism.
a: Dominant hormone of the fed state; lowers plasma glucose by increasing uptake and promoting storage (anabolism).

q: Explain the role of glucagon in the control of metabolism.
a: Dominant hormone of the fasted state; raises plasma glucose by stimulating glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis (catabolism).

q: Describe the ratio of insulin and glucagon in both the fed and fasted states.
a: Fed: High insulin/low glucagon ratio. Fasted: Low insulin/high glucagon ratio. The balance determines the metabolic direction.

q: Define the keywords: hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia.
a: Hypoglycemia: Low blood sugar. Hyperglycemia: High blood sugar.

q: Explain the different stimuli that influence insulin secretion.
a: Increased plasma glucose, increased plasma amino acids, GI hormones (GLP-1, GIP), and Parasympathetic activity. (Sympathetic inhibits).

q: Define the keywords: GLP-1, GIP, and incretin hormones.
a: GLP-1/GIP: Incretins; hormones secreted by the gut that stimulate insulin before glucose even rises in the blood.

q: Define the keywords: insulin-receptor substrates (IRS), alpha cells, and beta cells.
a: IRS: Signal proteins inside cells. Alpha cells: Produce glucagon. Beta cells: Produce insulin.

q: Explain the different ways that insulin lowers plasma glucose levels.
a: Increases transport into cells, enhances glucose utilization/storage, and inhibits the breakdown of glycogen/fat/proteins.

q: Explain the difference in GLUT transporters between the liver cells and other cells.
a: Adipose/Muscle use GLUT4 (insulin-dependent). Liver uses GLUT2 (always present), but insulin changes the direction of flow by managing internal concentrations.

q: Explain the different ways that glucagon raises plasma glucose levels.
a: Stimulates liver glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis; also promotes lipolysis to provide alternative energy.

q: Compare type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus.
a: Type 1: Insulin deficiency (beta cell destruction). Type 2: Insulin resistance (cells don't respond). Both result in hyperglycemia.

q: Define the keywords: osmotic diuresis, diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), and insulin resistance.
a: Osmotic diuresis: Glucose in urine pulling water with it. DKA: Metabolic acidosis from ketones. Resistance: Reduced response to insulin.

q: Describe the relationship between diabetes and ketoacidosis.
a: In the absence of insulin (Type 1), the body 'starves' and breaks down fat rapidly, creating excess ketones that lower blood pH.

q: Understand which neurohormones are produced by the posterior pituitary.
a: Oxytocin (labor/milk) and Vasopressin/ADH (water balance). Made in hypothalamus, stored/released from posterior pituitary.

q: Understand which neurohormones are produced by the anterior pituitary.
a: Prolactin, GH, TSH, ACTH, LH, and FSH. Controlled by hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones.

q: Define the keywords: releasing hormones, inhibiting hormones, and trophic hormones.
a: Releasing: Start secretion. Inhibiting: Stop secretion. Trophic: Hormones that control the secretion of other hormones.

q: Diagnose primary endocrine pathologies.
a: The problem is in the last endocrine gland in the pathway (e.g., the adrenal cortex itself).

q: Diagnose secondary endocrine pathologies.
a: The problem is in a trophic tissue (e.g., the anterior pituitary or hypothalamus) that controls the final gland.

q: Explain the possible causes of endocrine pathologies: Hypersecretion.
a: Excess hormone; often caused by tumors (adenomas) or medical treatments (iatrogenic).

q: Explain the possible causes of endocrine pathologies: Hyposecretion.
a: Deficient hormone; often caused by gland atrophy or dietary deficiencies (e.g., iodine).

q: Explain the possible causes of endocrine pathologies: Abnormal tissue responsiveness.
a: Hormone levels are normal, but receptors are missing, non-functional, or down-regulated (e.g., Type 2 Diabetes).

q: Explain the roles of hormone receptors and their role in cellular responses.
a: Receptors bind specific hormones to trigger signal transduction. Locations: Surface (peptides) or Intracellular (steroids).

q: Describe the roles of up- and down-regulation of receptors.
a: Down-regulation: Decrease in receptors due to chronic high hormone levels. Up-regulation: Increase in receptors to become more sensitive.

q: List the types of steroid hormones secreted by the adrenal cortex.
a: Aldosterone (mineralocorticoids), Cortisol (glucocorticoids), and Sex hormones (androgens).

q: Diagram the HPA pathway in detail.
a: Hypothalamus (CRH) -> Anterior Pituitary (ACTH) -> Adrenal Cortex (Cortisol). Cortisol provides negative feedback to both.

q: Describe the effects of cortisol in the body, especially on metabolism.
a: Protective against hypoglycemia; promotes gluconeogenesis, protein breakdown, and lipolysis. It is also immunosuppressive.

q: Identify the hallmarks of hypercortisolism (Cushing's).
a: Hyperglycemia, muscle wasting, 'moon face', and abdominal fat deposits with thin limbs.

q: Identify the hallmarks of hypocortisolism (Addison's).
a: Hypoglycemia, fatigue, and hyperpigmentation (due to high ACTH levels).

q: Identify the difference between primary and secondary hypercortisolism.
a: Primary: Adrenal tumor (High Cortisol, Low ACTH). Secondary: Pituitary tumor/Cushing's Disease (High Cortisol, High ACTH).

q: Explain the origins of T3 and T4 in the thyroid.
a: Produced in thyroid follicles using iodine and tyrosine. T4 is more abundant; T3 is more biologically active.

q: Define the following keywords: follicles, colloid, and iodine.
a: Follicles: Spherical structures in thyroid. Colloid: Sticky fluid in follicles containing thyroglobulin. Iodine: Essential element for thyroid hormone.

q: Define the keywords: TBG and deiodinases.
a: TBG: Transport protein in blood. Deiodinases: Enzymes in target cells that convert T4 to the active T3.

q: Diagram the thyroid hormone control pathway.
a: Hypothalamus (TRH) -> Anterior Pituitary (TSH) -> Thyroid (T3/T4). T3/T4 provide negative feedback.

q: Identify the hallmarks and side effects of hyperthyroidism.
a: Increased metabolic rate, heat intolerance, weight loss, and rapid heart rate. Example: Graves' disease.

q: Identify the hallmarks and side effects of hypothyroidism.
a: Slow metabolic rate, cold intolerance, weight gain, and slow heart rate/reflexes.

q: Identify the keywords: goiter, Graves' disease, TSI, and exophthalmos.
a: Goiter: Enlarged thyroid. Graves': Hyperthyroidism caused by TSI. TSI: Antibodies that mimic TSH. Exophthalmos: Bulging eyes.

q: Distinguish between primary and secondary thyroid pathologies.
a: Primary: Problem in thyroid gland. Secondary: Problem in pituitary (TSH) or hypothalamus (TRH).

q: List the factors that influence normal growth.
a: Growth hormone, thyroid hormones, insulin, adequate diet, and absence of chronic stress.

q: Diagram the control pathway for growth hormone release.
a: Hypothalamus (GHRH/SS) -> Anterior Pituitary (GH) -> Liver (IGFs). IGFs and GH provide feedback.

q: Define the keywords: GHRH, SS, and IGFs.
a: GHRH: Stimulates GH. SS (Somatostatin): Inhibits GH. IGFs: Insulin-like growth factors; mediate many of GH's growth effects.

q: Identify the hallmarks of hypersecretion of GH in children.
a: Giantism: Excessive height and growth due to stimulation of epiphyseal plates before they close.

q: Identify the hallmarks of hypersecretion of GH in adults.
a: Acromegaly: Coarsening of facial features and growth of hands/feet (plates are already closed).

q: Identify the hallmarks of hyposecretion of GH in children.
a: Dwarfism: Failure to reach normal height; often due to GH deficiency or receptor defects.
